evidence_quotes.quote: deliberate shutdown of legally purchased digital video games.
pressuring platforms to remove certain types of content, often video games, that are completely legal to sell and purchase.

paragraphs.text: As a lifelong supporter of digital innovation and consumer rights, I strongly support the goals of this Act, especially its intent to protect users from manipulative design and unfair commercial practices. However, I feel there are two major areas that also need to be addressed if the Act is to be truly comprehensive and future-proof. First, I want to highlight the growing public concern around the deliberate shutdown of legally purchased digital video games. This isnt about piracy or outdated tech, its about games that people paid for being rendered completely unusable because publishers or platforms decide to pull the plug. Consumers who paid for these products are left with nothing. Thats not just unfair, its a breach of trust. The recent Stop Destroying Video Games initiative, which gained over a million signatures across the EU, clearly shows how many people care about this. If someone buys a digital game, they should have the right to access it, whether that means an offline mode or the ability to run it on private servers. This is about preserving value, access, and culture, not about asking companies to support their products forever. Secondly, Im deeply concerned about the growing influence of payment processors like Visa and Mastercard in dictating what kinds of legal digital goods can be sold online. These companies have started pressuring platforms to remove certain types of content, often video games, that are completely legal to sell and purchase. When a financial gatekeeper can block access to a product simply because they disagree with its theme or content, thats not regulation, its censorship by proxy. No private company should have the power to control legal commerce, especially in digital markets. If a game or product is legal to sell under national law, it should not be up to Visa, Mastercard, or any other payment authority to decide whether people can access it. This overreach undermines both free enterprise and consumer autonomy. I understand these two concerns, digital product shutdowns and payment platform censorship, may not have been the initial focus of the Digital Fairness Act. But they absolutely fall under the same principles the Act is trying to protect: fairness, transparency, and respect for the consumer. I urge you to consider these areas seriously during the next stages of drafting. Addressing these issues would show that the Act truly understands the complexities of todays digital environment, and that it stands with the everyday consumer. Thank you for your time and for your efforts in bringing much-needed fairness to the digital world.
